body_txt: Red things just seem to go faster. In fiction, make something vermilion and you can almost guarantee it'll move quicker, as if the color itself reduces drag, lowers inertia, and increases forward momentum . It's a visual shorthand for getting the point across that an object is zippy. Red is the color of fire , violence , and passion , but it can also mean "caution" and "stop!" The specific link between red and speed seems to have been codified in the early days of auto racing, when race cars were painted according to their national origin to help fans root for their countrymen from the stands. Italy chose red, specifically rosso corsa (racing red), as their car color. For decades, Italian manufacturers were a dominant force on the racetrack. Corresponding to this racing tradition, companies like Ferrari, Alpha Romeo, Maserati, and Fiat created bold, stylish, and powerful sports cars for the road, usually painted red. It didn't take long for other car makers to start using crimson paint to help their own fast cars turn heads. Since then, red has become stamped into the public consciousness as the color of speed. Sub-Trope of Law of Chromatic Superiority and Color-Coded for Your Convenience . Compare Gold-Colored Superiority for another color that is considered greater than others (sometimes even red).
